cases.title: First on Scene at the Market Explosion
cases.presentation: You are the first EMS unit at an explosion in a crowded market. Smoke is rising, dozens of people are down or fleeing, and bystanders scream for you to help a bleeding man at your feet.
cases.question: What is your correct first sequence of actions?
options: A: Immediately treat the bleeding man in front of you.
B: Size up and report (detection), establish and announce incident command, address safety/security and hazards — before individual patient care.
C: Drive the nearest casualty to the hospital.
D: Wait outside the area until the police arrive.
cases.explanation: The DISASTER Paradigm runs in order: detection (size-up and report), incident command, safety/security, and hazard assessment come before touching the first patient. At a bombing, secondary devices make staging discipline and scene control lifesaving for responders — and effective command saves more victims than one-at-a-time care.
cases.clinicalPearl: The hardest BDLS skill is stepping past the first casualty to run the checklist — command and safety save more lives than immediate hands-on care.

cases.title: Everyone to Mercy General
cases.presentation: At a highway pile-up MCI with 25 casualties, transporting crews begin taking every patient to Mercy General, 5 minutes away. Two other hospitals, 15 and 20 minutes away, have received no patients. Mercy's ED calls: they are saturated.
cases.question: What has gone wrong, and what should command do?
options: A: Nothing — nearest hospital is always correct.
B: Evacuation failed: casualties were not distributed. Command should assign destinations by acuity across all appropriate receiving facilities.
C: Mercy General should simply work faster.
D: Stop all transports until Mercy clears.
cases.explanation: Sending every casualty to the closest facility relocates the disaster into its ED. The evacuation component requires command-directed distribution: match patient acuity to facility capability and spread the load across the system.
cases.clinicalPearl: An MCI transported badly becomes a hospital MCI — distribution is a command function, not a driver's choice.

cases.title: The Second Wave
cases.presentation: Twenty minutes into a response at an industrial fire, crews have settled into patient care. A responder notices a storage tank near the treatment area beginning to vent vapor. The initial hazard assessment had cleared this area.
cases.question: What does this development demonstrate?
options: A: The initial assessment was negligent.
B: Hazard assessment is continuous — scenes evolve, so the treatment area must be relocated and command notified immediately.
C: Vapor clouds are harmless once patients are triaged.
D: Only the safety officer may notice hazards.
cases.explanation: The 'Assess hazards' component never ends: an area safe on arrival can become dangerous. Any responder who spots a new hazard reports it at once; command relocates operations and updates the safety perimeter.
cases.clinicalPearl: Your scene size-up has an expiration time — keep looking up from the patient.
